blurb: A silvery-leaved shortgrass-prairie native studding low spreading mats with apricot-scarlet cup flowers all summer — bone-dry tough and a reliable native-bee plant for the harshest, leanest ground.
spread: Spreads by rhizome into low colonies
seasons: Apricot-orange cup flowers late spring–summer; gray-green lobed foliage

care.hardiness: A widespread shortgrass-prairie native, hardy well below Boulder's zone 5b–6a; it tolerates our cold, heat, alkaline soil, and drought equally.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in spring after the mid-May last frost or in early fall, in lean, sharply drained soil. It has a deep taproot, so set out young plants and don't disturb established ones; in containers give it a gritty mineral mix and keep it dry.[2][3]
care.sun: Full sun. In shade the leaves turn thinner, less lobed, and bright green and the plant flowers poorly, so give it the open, hot exposure it evolved for.[1][2]
care.soil: Light, gravelly, sandy or limestone/clay soils with sharp drainage; it thrives on lean, alkaline, calcareous ground and dislikes rich or wet soil.[1]
care.water: Very low; xeric. Deeply drought-tolerant thanks to a taproot that can run unbranched ~3 ft before branching — needs essentially no supplemental water once established.[2][3]
care.spacing: Space about 12–18 inches apart; it spreads by rhizomes and self-seeding into colonies, so it knits together over time.[2]
care.propagation: By seed or rhizome cuttings. Seed has a hard coat — scarification helps, especially on dried, stored seed. It also spreads by rhizomes, so rhizome cuttings can be taken in spring, though the deep taproot makes moving mature plants difficult.[1][2]
care.sow: Sow scarified seed; germination is improved by scarification, particularly for stored seed.[1]
care.bloom: Long bloom from mid-spring into fall — roughly April/May through September — in profuse orange to brick-red cupped flowers about 1 inch wide.[1][2]
care.maintenance: Very low; leave it to spread, and avoid overwatering or rich soil. Wear gloves and eye protection when handling or collecting seed (see cautions).[2]
care.selfsow: Spreads by both rhizomes and self-seeding, forming clumps or extensive colonies — give it room or contain it.[2]
care.troubles: Few problems; rots only in wet, rich soil. Its stellate leaf hairs can irritate skin and eyes during handling (see edible/caution).[2][3]

edible.summary: No documented culinary use; grown as a xeric ornamental, and the leaf hairs are a skin/eye irritant.[1][3]
edible.caution: No authoritative food use is documented, so this guide treats it as inedible — don't eat it. The whole plant is covered in fine star-shaped (stellate) hairs that can irritate skin and especially eyes (its folk names 'sore-eye poppy' / 'mal de ojo' come from this), and handling or collecting seed has caused dermatitis and eye irritation — wear gloves and don't rub your eyes after touching it.[3]
